title: Ear and Eye Irrigation
subtitle: The nurse irrigates safely — warming ear solution and pulling the pinna up and back, withholding for a perforated eardrum, and flushing the eye from inner to outer canthus, copiously and at once for a chemical splash.

patient.name: Ear / Eye Irrigation
patient.admitDate: The nurse performs ear and eye irrigation to remove cerumen, debris, or a chemical.

vitals.temp: No ear irrigation if TM perforated

labs.v: Toward the side of the canal.

nursesNote: The nurse performs ear and eye irrigation. Ear irrigation removes cerumen or debris; the solution is warmed to body temperature because cold or hot fluid against the eardrum causes dizziness, vertigo, and nausea. The pinna is pulled up and back in an adult (down and back in a child under three) to straighten the canal, and the stream is directed toward the side of the canal rather than at the eardrum, with the return allowed to flow out into a basin. Ear irrigation is withheld if the tympanic membrane is perforated or an ear infection is suspected. Eye irrigation flushes a foreign body or chemical from the eye; for a chemical splash the eye is flushed immediately and copiously with sterile normal saline (at least 15 minutes) before other care. The head is turned toward the affected side so fluid flows from the inner to the outer canthus into a basin, away from the unaffected eye, and the stream is aimed onto the conjunctiva, not directly on the cornea. The nurse stops either irrigation for new pain, dizziness, or nausea, then assesses and documents.
providerOrders: Warm ear irrigation solution to body temperature
Pull the pinna up and back (adult) or down and back (young child)
Withhold ear irrigation if the eardrum is perforated or infected
Flush a chemical eye exposure immediately and copiously with saline
Irrigate the eye from inner to outer canthus into a basin

Eye fluid flows from the inner to the outer canthus

Pulling the pinna straightens the ear canal
Directing flow at the eardrum can injure it
Flowing from inner to outer canthus protects the other eye
A chemical eye exposure is flushed immediately and copiously
Ear irrigation is the first step if an ear infection is suspected

Warm the solution to body temperature
Position the patient with a basin under the ear
Pull the pinna up and back and direct flow to the canal side
Stop for pain/dizziness; assess return and document
Pull the pinna up and back in an adult
Flow fluid from the inner to the outer canthus
Withhold if the eardrum is perforated
Flush a chemical splash immediately and copiously
Warm the solution to avoid dizziness and vertigo
Direct the stream onto the conjunctiva, not the cornea
Aim toward the side of the canal, not the eardrum
Turn the head toward the affected side over a basin

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: During ear irrigation the patient reports sudden ear pain and dizziness
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stop the irrigation immediately
Assess the ear, pain, and for vertigo or nausea
Keep the patient still and safe from falling
Notify the provider
Document the findings
Warming the irrigation solution to body temperature
Using a cold solution for an ear irrigation
Pulling the pinna up and back in an adult
Irrigating an ear with a known perforated eardrum
Flushing a chemical eye splash at once with copious saline
Flowing eye irrigant from the outer toward the inner canthus
Directing eye fluid onto the conjunctiva into a basin
Continuing irrigation despite new pain and dizziness
